# What is a serial interval?

## Two clocks, often confused

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
- **Serial interval (SI)**: time between *symptom onset* of infector and infectee
- **Generation interval (GI)**: time between *infections*
- The SI can be **negative** when transmission happens before symptoms
- During epidemic growth the *observed* SI **contracts**, so it is not fixed

::: {.callout-note}
The GI is what transmission models need.
The SI is what we usually get to observe.
:::
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
![](figures/fig-gt-vs-si-timeline.png){width="100%"}

::: {.attribution}
Source: within-host-informed event-time framework (Abbott et al., working paper, no public URL yet)
:::
:::
::::

## Why do we want it?

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
- Feeds $R_t$ via the renewal equation
- Links growth rate $r$ to reproduction number $R$
- Informs forecasting and contact tracing
- But it is a **proxy** for the generation interval, the quantity transmission models actually need
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
::: {.callout-note}
Defined from the right reference cohort, the forward SI links $r$ to $R$ just as the GI does, and the observed SI shortens during growth.
:::

::: {.attribution}
Source: [Park et al. (2021)](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.2011548118); [Wallinga & Lipsitch (2007)](https://doi.org/10.1098/rspb.2006.3754); [Lehtinen et al. (2021)](https://doi.org/10.1098/rsif.2020.0756)
:::
:::
::::

## A serial interval is just a delay

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
- Estimating an SI = estimating a **time-to-event distribution** from incomplete data
- A special case of the survival-analysis problem this room already knows
- Onset times are **interval-censored** (daily data)
- Real-time data are **right-truncated** (the nowcasting problem)
- The twist: transmission makes event times **dependent**, and growth tilts the observed delays
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
::: {.callout-note}
Roll back the framing: the SI is one delay among many (incubation, onset-to-report, onset-to-death).
The hard part is the censoring and truncation, not the biology.
:::

::: {.attribution}
Source: [Charniga et al. (2024)](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pcbi.1012520); [Park et al. (2024)](https://doi.org/10.1101/2024.01.12.24301247)
:::
:::
::::

## How should we estimate and report delays?

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
- **Adjust** for censoring (always), right truncation and dynamical bias (when needed)
- **Report uncertainty**: credible/confidence intervals, ideally joint posterior samples
- **Report** mean and spread, key quantiles, and the fitted distribution
- **Check** negative SIs and uncertain infector pairs
- **Be transparent**: share de-identified line lists and documented code
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
::: {.callout-note}
Charniga et al. (2024) give a checklist and flowchart for estimating and reporting epidemiological delays, with the SI and incubation period as worked examples.
:::

::: {.attribution}
Source: [Charniga et al. (2024)](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pcbi.1012520)
:::
:::
::::

## The method landscape

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
- Many methods exist for the **same** delay-estimation problem
- Ignoring censoring, truncation and growth-phase bias gives **divergent, biased** estimates
- Naive serial-interval fits can bias initial $R$ by up to a factor of **2.6**
- Standardised, bias-aware methods make results **comparable**
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
::: {.callout-note}
Park et al. (2024) review estimation of epidemiological delays and show that overlooked truncation, interval censoring and dynamical bias drive much of the disagreement between published estimates.
:::

::: {.attribution}
Source: [Park et al. (2024)](https://doi.org/10.1101/2024.01.12.24301247); [Park et al. (2021)](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.2011548118)
:::
:::
::::
